description: Iraqi invasion of Kuwait removed a major OPEC producer's exports from the market and raised fears of a wider Gulf conflict, sparking a sharp price spike.

event_name: Start of Gulf War (Operation Desert Storm)
description: US-led coalition began military action to expel Iraqi forces from Kuwait; oil markets had already priced in much of the risk, and prices fell once war began ('sell the rumor, buy the fact').
Collapse of the Thai baht triggered a regional financial crisis that sharply reduced Asian oil demand growth expectations through 1998.

event_name: 1998 oil price collapse (OPEC oversupply + Asian crisis)
description: Combination of weak Asian demand and OPEC production increases pushed Brent to under $10/barrel, one of the lowest real prices in decades.
OPEC and non-OPEC producers (including Mexico, Norway) agreed to coordinated output cuts, helping prices recover through 1999-2000.
Attacks on the US raised concerns about global economic slowdown and air travel demand, contributing to short-term price weakness amid broader market turmoil.
Invasion raised concerns about supply disruption from a major OPEC producer, adding a geopolitical risk premium to prices during the buildup and early war.
Combination of strong emerging-market demand, a weak US dollar, and speculative activity drove Brent to an all-time high before the global financial crisis unwound demand.
Collapse of Lehman Brothers triggered a global financial crisis and demand collapse, driving Brent from record highs to roughly $40/barrel within months.
Political uprisings across the Middle East and North Africa, including the loss of Libyan production during civil war, disrupted supply and lifted prices above $100/barrel.

event_name: OPEC declines to cut production amid oversupply
description: Facing rising US shale output, OPEC (led by Saudi Arabia) chose to defend market share rather than price, refusing to cut output despite falling prices, accelerating the 2014-2016 price collapse.

event_name: OPEC+ agrees to first coordinated output cut since 2008
description: OPEC, joined by Russia and other non-OPEC producers (forming 'OPEC+'), agreed to cut production to address the prolonged supply glut, supporting a price recovery.

event_name: US withdraws from Iran nuclear deal, reimposes sanctions
description: Renewed US sanctions on Iranian oil exports tightened expected supply and added a geopolitical risk premium to prices through late 2018.

event_name: Drone attacks on Saudi Aramco Abqaiq facility
description: Attacks temporarily knocked out roughly half of Saudi Arabia's oil production capacity, causing the largest single-day price spike in Brent's history.
Talks between OPEC and Russia collapsed, and Saudi Arabia launched a price war by ramping up production and slashing prices, coinciding with collapsing COVID-19 demand.

event_name: COVID-19 demand collapse (US WTI turns negative)
description: Global lockdowns collapsed oil demand; US WTI futures briefly traded negative due to storage constraints, and Brent fell to multi-decade lows.
OPEC+ agreed to cut roughly 9.7 million barrels per day, the largest coordinated cut in history, to stabilize prices amid the COVID-19 demand shock.
Invasion triggered sanctions on Russian energy exports and fears of a major supply disruption from one of the world's largest oil exporters, pushing Brent above $100/barrel.

event_name: OPEC+ announces large production cut amid price weakness
description: OPEC+ agreed to cut output by 2 million barrels per day to support prices amid recession fears and falling demand expectations.
Saudi Arabia and other OPEC+ members announced surprise voluntary production cuts to shore up prices amid banking-sector turmoil and demand concerns.
